Why did the inner planets become rocky instead of gaseous?

The inner planets formed close to the hot young Sun, where metals and silicate rock could condense but most ices and light gases could not stay. That left Mercury, Venus, Earth, and Mars as compact rocky worlds instead of gas-rich giants.

Quick quiz: was the inner Solar System too hot or too cold for many ices to condense?
Too hot is the correct idea. Near the young Sun, many volatile ices and light gases could not condense easily, while rock and metal grains could gather into the terrestrial planets.

Why are the outer planets so different from Mercury through Mars?

Farther from the Sun, the Solar System was cooler, so ices could condense and growing cores could capture much more gas. That is why the outer planets became giant worlds while Mercury, Venus, Earth, and Mars stayed rocky.

Explain the frost line without making it too technical.

The frost line is the distance from the young Sun where it became cool enough for ices to condense. Inside that warmer region, rock and metal dominated; beyond it, icy material helped build the giant planets and many small bodies.

The learner previously mixed up hot and cold formation zones. Correct that clearly.

Here is the clean version: the inner Solar System was hotter, so mostly metals and silicate rocks condensed there. The outer Solar System was cooler, allowing more ices and gases to join planet formation.

Why did Jupiter and Saturn collect so much gas?

Jupiter and Saturn formed farther from the Sun where cooler conditions allowed more solid icy material to build large cores. Once those cores grew massive enough, their gravity captured huge envelopes of hydrogen and helium gas.

Which materials survived best near the young Sun?

Rock-forming silicates and metals survived best near the young Sun because they could remain solid at high temperatures. Lighter gases and many ices were much harder to keep in the hot inner Solar System.

Compare Earth and Neptune using formation temperature.

Earth formed in the warmer inner Solar System, where rock and metal dominated. Neptune formed much farther out in a colder region, where ices were available and the planet could become a giant rich in volatiles.

What is the safest short answer for why inner planets are rocky?
The safest short answer is: they formed close to the hot young Sun, where rock and metal could condense but many gases and ices could not. That made the inner planets dense, solid, and rocky.

Quick quiz: name the four rocky inner planets and why they are rocky.

The four rocky inner planets are Mercury, Venus, Earth, and Mars. They are rocky because they formed in the hot inner Solar System, where metals and silicate rocks condensed more readily than ices or light gases.
